section_a___language_background.primary_languages_spoken_in_home: Vietnamese and English. At home we primarily use Vietnamese when speaking with grandparents and during family meals, prayers, and cultural celebrations (Tet, Mid-Autumn Festival). We also use English regularly between the parents and the children when discussing schoolwork, reading books together, or watching movies. I would estimate that overall we speak Vietnamese about 60% of the time and English about 40% of the time in the home environment.
section_a___language_background.language_child_uses_most_frequently: English. Minh uses English most of the time with his older sister, with friends at school, when playing online games with classmates, and when talking about school subjects such as math, science, and reading. He naturally switches into English when he is excited or when he is explaining something he learned in class. With extended family on video calls, he sometimes starts in English and then switches to simpler Vietnamese phrases when prompted.
section_a___language_background.language_child_learned_first: Vietnamese. Minh’s first words and early phrases were in Vietnamese because he spent most of his time with his grandmother from birth until about age three. Common early words included “ba” (dad), “me” (mom), “an com” (eat rice), and “nuoc” (water). We introduced some English words (colors, numbers, and animal names) around age three, but Vietnamese remained the primary language up until he started preschool.
section_a___language_background.language_used_most_with_child: Vietnamese at home for daily routines and family conversations, and English for homework and school-related topics. For example, we speak Vietnamese when reminding him to brush his teeth, eat dinner, or help with chores, and when telling stories about our family in Vietnam. We switch to English when going over his reading assignments, helping with math word problems, or discussing school rules and behavior expectations so that the vocabulary matches what he hears in class.
section_a___language_background.how_long_attended_school_in_us: 4 years and 6 months (started kindergarten in August 2019 in San Jose, CA, and has continuously attended public school in the U.S. since then, now entering 4th grade in Centerville City Schools).
section_a___language_background.languages_of_instruction: English has been the primary language of instruction in all of Minh’s schools in the United States. In kindergarten through 3rd grade, all core subjects (reading, writing, math, science, social studies) were taught in English. Additionally, his previous school offered a short weekly Vietnamese heritage language club after school where basic reading and writing in Vietnamese were introduced, but this was enrichment and not the main instructional language. At home, we sometimes use Vietnamese-language children’s books and YouTube educational videos (such as Vietnamese alphabet songs and simple science explanations), so he has had some informal exposure to Vietnamese as a language of learning as well.
section_a___language_background.additional_information_english_language_:
Minh has had a gradual and fairly natural transition from Vietnamese-dominant speech at home in his toddler years to English-dominant speech now that he is in elementary school. When he started kindergarten, his receptive English was already fairly strong because he had watched English-language children’s shows (like "Paw Patrol" and "Octonauts") and listened to his older sister speak English with her friends. However, he was initially shy about speaking in full English sentences with adults, especially his teachers.

In kindergarten and 1st grade, his teachers noted that he sometimes mixed Vietnamese and English in the same sentence, especially when he did not know a specific English word (for example, saying something like, “Can I have more nuoc?” instead of “Can I have more water?”). Over time, with more exposure and encouragement, he has almost completely stopped code-switching in the classroom and now uses age-appropriate English vocabulary for everyday situations.

We read with him in English every night for about 20–30 minutes, focusing on both fiction and nonfiction books from the public library. He especially enjoys books about space, dinosaurs, and graphic novels like "Dog Man." When we read, we pause to explain new vocabulary words and ask him to retell what happened in the story in his own words. This has helped him build stronger comprehension skills and confidence in expressing his thoughts in English.

During the COVID-19 remote learning period, Minh used English extensively for Zoom classes, online assignments (Google Classroom, Raz-Kids, and Epic), and video chats with classmates. This daily practice improved his listening comprehension, ability to follow multi-step directions in English, and comfort with speaking up to ask questions. His teachers have commented that his pronunciation is clear and that he now speaks English similarly to his native-English-speaking peers.

At the same time, we have tried to maintain his Vietnamese so he can communicate with grandparents and extended family members who do not speak English. We ask him to respond in Vietnamese when talking to his grandparents on FaceTime, and we use Vietnamese at family gatherings and during cultural celebrations. He understands more Vietnamese than he actively speaks and sometimes searches for the right Vietnamese word, but he shows interest in learning and often asks us, “How do I say this in Vietnamese?”

Overall, Minh functions comfortably in English in academic settings and social situations. He can follow classroom instructions without translation, participate in group projects, and understand grade-level texts with occasional support for more advanced vocabulary. At home, we continue to support both languages so that he can maintain his cultural heritage while fully participating in school and community life in English.

section_b___communication_preferences.language_for_written_information: English. We are comfortable receiving school emails, letters, newsletters, report cards, and other written notices in English. If there are very important documents such as legal notices or special education evaluations, we would appreciate knowing if a Vietnamese translation is available, but English is acceptable as the primary written communication language.
section_b___communication_preferences.language_for_oral_information: English. Both parents can communicate in English over the phone and during in-person meetings. The mother (Lan Nguyen) is more comfortable with everyday conversation in English, while the father (Huy Nguyen) sometimes prefers slower speech and occasional clarification for more technical or academic terms. If a situation is complex (for example, special education meetings or detailed testing results), having the option of a Vietnamese interpreter would be helpful, but it is not strictly required for regular school communication.
